feed.title: SAP Community - SAP Datasphere
feed.subtitle: SAP Datasphere blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: I tricked Datasphere into running my custom scaler functions
entry.content:
Introduction - Welcome, once again. 

Hello, and welcome to an easter edition of my hit-series of blogposts, where I try to help my fellow developers get to grips with SAP Datasphere .

Easter is over, but today I've put on my Reverse-Easter-Bunny costume, and have dug up a few golden easter eggs for you. 
Even if you think the word "Scalar Functions" sounds like scary techno-babble, I still think there is something of values in here, that you might find useful.

And do not worry, if you are hoping for my usual sprinkling of sarcasm or was hoping I would make fun of SAP and their interesting design decisions, you will not be disappointed. My God, are we going to cry. I mean laugh. Laugh. Yes. 

Today we will be going through the following:

  1. What is a Database User Group, and why you should learn to love it.
  2. How to re-enable SYS.DUMMY functionality (because shouldn't we?)
  3. How to build and implement custom scalar functions across your Datasphere solution
  4. Jail-break the Graphical View editor, so we can gas-light it into doing its f#!&ing job.

Happy reading 🙂 

1: Why you should always create a "Database User Group" 

So, if you have ever been in an hour-long townhall meeting, you might have mindlessly scrolled through the Datasphere documentation (Only me? Alright then), in hope of stumbling over something good. 

On one such occasion, I came across how to create a database user group, and thought myself: 

"What the hell is that? Why should I care?"

In the documentation, we get the following:

"Users with an administrator role can create database user groups in SAP Datasphere to allow users to work in a sandboxed area in the underlying SAP HANA Cloud database, unattached to any space. These users can transfer an existing data warehouse implementation into the SAP Datasphere database or do any other work in SAP HANA Cloud and then make it available to one or more spaces as appropriate"

Which is a pretty boring few sentences, but here is the key value: You can create database objects, that can be accessed across your solution. 
This allows you to use the full suite (almost, let's not get ahead of ourselves) of HANA SQL functionality, which can offer a lot more programmability than Datasphere in general. 

1.1: Setup your Database User Group  

You can follow the documentation to create the "Admin" user for your group. 
Open the "Database Explorer", provide the password, and open the SQL console.

Note: I've used CHR_GRA as the namespace in my examples, you can use whatever fits you.

In your SQL console, you will now have to:

  • Create a non-admin user
  • Create a schema 
-- Step 1: Create User, Schema and role.
CREATE USER DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<USER> PASSWORD Password123 SET USERGROUP DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>;
CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."CREATE_USERGROUP_SCHEMA"
(
	SCHEMA_NAME => 'DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<SCHEMA_NAME>',
	OWNER_NAME => 'DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<USER>'
					);

Technical side-note: By default, HANA creates a unique schema for each user. So it's good practise to create a separate schema, that is independent of any user. 

1.2: Switch user

In the left side of the Database Explorer, right-click on your database and select "Add Database with Different User":

Use the user ID and password you created above. 

Open a new SQL console, and validate that you are now connected to your user:

1.3: Cool... Now what?

Now you can do whatever you want. 
You can create custom tables, views, functions and procedures in the schema you set up and expose it to the spaces in your solutions. 

2: Re-enabling SYS.DUMMY

So, if you've ever read any of the documentation for HANA SQL, you'll see endless references to a table called DUMMY. 

An example could be getting today's date

SELECT current_date
FROM sys.dummy;

Which may seem odd, but the (somewhat simple) explanation is that HANA will not execute a query without a source. 
So, the table SYS.DUMMY only ever contains one row, so you can use it as a workaround, for cases where you don't want to query data, but needs to execute something else. 

In SAP Datasphere , the above query will fail, if you try it in an SQL view:

But hell, sometimes you just want a query without a source. So, how can we re-establish it? 

In our database your schema, we can create a dummy view, and expose it in our space:

SET SCHEMA DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<SCHEMA_NAME>; 

CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW "DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<SCHEMA_NAME>"."DUMMY" AS SELECT "DUMMY" from "SYS"."DUMMY";

Which creates a wrapper view. 
Why is that required? Because your spaces will always try to execute an SQL query inside it's own schema, and will not be able to find the SYS schema. 

When the view is created, you'll have to grant access to your space by running the following SQL:

CALL "DWC_GLOBAL"."GRANT_PRIVILEGE_TO_SPACE" (
	OPERATION => 'GRANT', 
	PRIVILEGE => 'SELECT', 
	SCHEMA_NAME => 'DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<SCHEMA_NAME>', 
	OBJECT_NAME => '', 
SPACE_ID => '<YOUR_SPACE>');

 In your Datasphere space, you can now deploy the view, and you're back in action!

"But Christian, can't I just create a local table in my space and do the exact same thing?"

Well yes. But I think this served as a nice example of how you can use your DB user group to access system information, that is not otherwise available to you. 

3: Implementing custom scalar functions 

Alrighty, so on to the real magic. 

If you are not familiar with SQL development, a bit of background might be useful. 

In SQL in general, and HANA SQL as well, you can build User-Defined Functions, that allow you to implement more complex SQL logic, such as control structures (loops) and logical operators, and execute dynamic SQL. HANA offers two kinds of User-Defined Functions that you can build.

  1. Table function: Which returns a full view, that you can call from a SELECT statement. 
  2. Scalar function: Which are functions you can call within a view, and returns a single value for each row.
    When you create a "Calculated column" in your views, you are using a scalar function. Functions such as IFNULL, COALESCE, LOWER and so on are all scalar functions, that you can use in your views. 

The value of scaler functions is that they allow you to create re-usable custom logic, that can be utilized across your entire solution. 
In this post, I'll create two functions as a few examples, which should give you an idea of how to utilize scalar functions in your solution. 

3.1: Example 1 - Harmonization of amount columns 

One challenge we always encounter in a data warehouse is the standardization of data formats when combining data from several sources. An example that may be very common now with the introduction of Seamless Planning, is combining planning data with actuals data from an ERP system. 

By default, measures from SAC will be decimals with 7 decimals; whereas actuals from wither BW or SAP will be a decimal with 2 decimals. When combining the numbers in Datasphere, it will do it's best, but it may lead to either a loss of precision, or an ungodly amount of decimals in your reports. 

So, what if you want to enforce a consistent data format across your solution? Well, you could use a scalar function. 

In this example, I have decided I want all my amounts to be a Decimal(17, 2). Why? Because I grew up with BW, and 17,2 just feels like the objectively correct format. You may disagree (and you would be objectively wrong) and choose another format. 

So, open your Database Explorer, and open an SQL console, and make sure it's connected to the user your created in step 1.1. 

First, set the correct schema:

SET SCHEMA DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<YOUR_SCHEMA>;

Then you can create your function as below:

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION clean_amount_column (
	in_amount DECIMAL
)
returns out_amount decimal(17,2) -- Defines return type
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
SQL SECURITY DEFINER -- Definer means it will use your DB user's rights, not whoever is using your report.
READS SQL DATA -- Makes sure the function is READ-ONLY. Functions almost always are, but it will help the compiler optimize function calls.
DETERMINISTIC -- Tells the compiler that the function will always return the same output, that means the results are cached for the runtime session, and can improve performance.

as 
BEGIN


DECLARE v_num DECIMAL(34,10);

    -- Null handling: If NULL, return 0. 
    IF in_amount IS NULL THEN
        out_amount := 0;
    ELSE 
		-- Convert to highest possible precision. Ensure we can handle scientific magnitude numbers. 
	    v_num := TO_DECIMAL(:in_amount, 34, 10);
		v_num := ROUND(v_num,2); -- Round to two decimals.
	    out_amount := TO_DECIMAL(:v_num, 17, 2); -- Convert to final DECIMAL(17,2)
    END IF;

END;

A few notes on the script:

  • CREATE OR REPLACE: If a function with that name, in that schema already exists, it will be overwritten. You may not want to accidently overwrite existing objects. In that case, you can use CREATE. 
  • SQL SECURITY DEFINER: This means that the function will run with the rights of your DB user, rather than the user calling. This may not be what you want, so change to Invoker if you need that instead.
  • DETERMINISTIC: Is something you can use if you can promise the server that your function will always return the same result, given the same input, which improves performance, because results can be cached. Functions that require NOW() or TODAY() or CURRENT_DATE can not be deterministic. 

When you have run the script, you should be able to find your function in your DB Schema:

You can right-click and select "Generate SELECT Statement", and test your function. 

SELECT 12.747456 as Input, '12.76' as ExptectedOutput, "DWCDBGROUP#CHR_GRA#REPORTING"."CLEAN_AMOUNT_COLUMN"(12.764456).OUT_AMOUNT AS OUT_AMOUNT FROM DUMMY
UNION
SELECT 124545678.767456 as Input, 124545678.77 as ExptectedOutput, "DWCDBGROUP#CHR_GRA#REPORTING"."CLEAN_AMOUNT_COLUMN"(124545678.767456).OUT_AMOUNT AS OUT_AMOUNT FROM DUMMY
UNION
SELECT NULL as Input, 0.00 as ExptectedOutput, "DWCDBGROUP#CHR_GRA#REPORTING"."CLEAN_AMOUNT_COLUMN"(NULL).OUT_AMOUNT AS OUT_AMOUNT FROM DUMMY
UNION
SELECT '123.678' as Input, '123.68' as ExptectedOutput, "DWCDBGROUP#CHR_GRA#REPORTING"."CLEAN_AMOUNT_COLUMN"('123.678').OUT_AMOUNT AS OUT_AMOUNT FROM DUMMY

I've tested with a few values, including NULL and even a string 

INPUTEXPTECTEDOUTPUTOUT_AMOUNT

NULL

00.00
123.678123.68123.68
12.74745612.7612.76
124545678.767456124545678.77124545678.77

When you are happy with your function, you need to provide access to your spaces. To do this you will need to:

  • Create a public synonym for your function
  • Grant EXECUTE rights to your space to functions in your schema. 

The following statement may not make sense, but the technical reasons why this works is way beyond the scope of this post. So you'll just have to trust me. 
If you want the technical breakdown; let me know, and I might include an appendix to this post at some point. 

-- Create a public synonym - DO NOT REPLACE any existing synonyms.
-- If you're a puritan, you can always add a Z to your function and / or synonym. 
CREATE PUBLIC SYNONYM ZCLEAN_AMOUNT for DWCDBGROUP#CHR_GRA#REPORTING.clean_amount_column;

The public synonym is the name your function will have in your views, so give it a name good name. 

Get the ID of the space you want to give access to. Then run the following commands in an SQL console:

GRANT SELECT, EXECUTE ON SCHEMA "DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<YOUR_SCHEMA>" TO "<SPACE_ID>"."<SPACE_ID>::default" WITH GRANT OPTION;
GRANT SELECT, EXECUTE ON SCHEMA "DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_NAMESPACE>#<YOUR_SCHEMA>" TO <SPACE_ID> WITH GRANT OPTION;

Note: the format is important. The quotes ( " ) must be there, otherwise you'll get errors. 

So, how do you implement it?

Back in Datasphere, go to the space you granted access to, and create an SQL view. 
You can now call your function by it's synonym, and feed it your inputs:

Important note: Datasphere, however, will not correctly derive the return type of your custom scalar functions, but will inherit  the datatype of your input. 
In my case, "Lines" is an integer, and the hard-coded values is a double, so I had to go in and manually change the format. 

"But Christian, if I have to manually adjust the data type anyway, what is the value here?"

Well, yeah, that is a pain. However, even so, I would still argue that things like consistent null-behaviour, string-conversions etc., still have value. 
Also, please remember that Datasphere dosen't know it actually supports customer scalar functions. This is basically me gaslighting it into running my functions, so a few quirks are to be expected. 

3.2: Example 2: Consistent Date-conversions

Another colossal pain in the backend in any data modelling scenario is how to manage dates.
One absolute banger of timewaster is to get the CALMONTH (YYYYMM) from a date. Year, month, week, etc. all have standard functions implemented, but CALMONTH does not. 
The formula is pretty simple, but so help me God, do I always manage to get it wrong, for some reason. (I am fully prepared to accept that the reason may be that I'm an idiot, but there is no reason to personal).

So, for this example, I'll show you another happy little easter egg: A scaler function can return several values, and can act like a class with different methods. 

In this example, we'll take a basic SAP datestring (YYYYMMDD) and provide a single function, that can return year, month, calmonth, calweek, halfyear and quarter. 
You can append it as you prefer. 

SET SCHEMA DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_SCHEMA>#<YOUR_SCHEMA>; 

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION transform_sap_date (
	in_datestring nvarchar(8)
)
returns 
	out_year nvarchar(4), --YYYY
	out_month nvarchar(2), -- MM
	out_calmonth nvarchar(6), --YYYYMM
	out_calweek nvarchar(6), -- YYYYWW
	out_quarter nvarchar(1), -- Q
	out_halfyear nvarchar(1) --H
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
SQL SECURITY DEFINER 
READS SQL DATA 
DETERMINISTIC 

as 
BEGIN

DECLARE lv_sql_date date;

    -- Null handling: If NULL, null. 
    IF in_datestring IS NULL or length(in_datestring) != 8 THEN
		out_year := null;
		out_month := null;
		out_calmonth := null;
		out_calweek := null;
		out_quarter := null;
		out_halfyear := null;
    ELSE 
    	lv_sql_date := to_date(:in_datestring);
		out_year := to_nvarchar(year(:lv_sql_date));
		out_month := lpad(month(:lv_sql_date),2,'0');
		out_calmonth := concat(:out_year, :out_month);
		out_calweek := concat(:out_year, lpad(week(:lv_sql_date),2,'0'));
		out_quarter := left(ROUND( month(:lv_sql_date) / 3, 0, ROUND_UP),1);
		IF month(:lv_sql_date) < 6 then
			out_halfyear :=  '1';
		else
			out_halfyear :=  '2' ;
		end if;
		
    END IF;

END;

-- Create a public synonym - DO NOT REPLACE any existing synonyms.
-- If you're a puritan, you can always add a Z to your function and / or synonym. 
CREATE PUBLIC SYNONYM ZDATE for DWCDBGROUP#<YOUR_SCHEMA>#<YOUR_SCHEMA>.transform_sap_date;

You can now test your function. You can get each output by specifying it with a .<IDENTIFYER> in each column:

SELECT 
	'20260408' as InputDate,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_YEAR AS OUT_YEAR,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_MONTH AS OUT_MONTH,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_CALMONTH AS OUT_CALMONTH,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_CALWEEK AS OUT_CALWEEK,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_QUARTER AS OUT_QUARTER,
	"ZDATE"('20260408').OUT_HALFYEAR AS OUT_HALFYEAR
FROM DUMMY

So, .OUT_YEAR gives you the year, and so on. Which gives you the following result:

Which is a great success! 

So, in your view, you can now pass a date or a datestring to a function, and get whatever you need:

Note: Again, DSP gets confused about the datatype, and wants to give me a string(100):

So you will have to change that, but you can't have it all. 

3.3: A few notes

Alrighty! So now we've seen how to create and implement custom, reusable logic across your Datasphere solution. 

But it is not without headaches. 
First and foremost, SQL artifacts can't be included in the transport packages, so you will have to repeat the process for each system in your landscape. 

So, when transporting, the sequence is: (1) Make sure all the spaces are created, (2) create your DB group, schema and user, (3) Create or replace all the SQL objects, (4) Import your Datasphere views and models.

4: Jailbreaking the Graphical Editor

So, you may have noticed that up until now I've created SQL views to test my functions. 
The reason is quite simple: The Graphical editor has access to fewer functions than the SQL editor.

However, that is easily fixed, if you are willing to fiddle with the debug console a bit. 

Note: This works now, but technically it is a bit on the naughty side, so SAP may patch it out in the future. 
However, it they patch it with the same speed that the add new features, I'm guessing this should make it to at least 2027Q3 before they notice. 

So, the "allowed functions" in the graphical viewer is loaded once as a static list, the first time the editor is opened. 

When you "Validate" and "Deploy" your view, the functions are validated against the static list, before it is deployed. If a function is not in that list, the view will not deploy. 

So, the obvious fix is to smack in a breakpoint when the list is loaded, manually add your functions, and then deploy the view as always. 

Note: This process must be repeated every time a view is opened, and must be done when the editor initially loads. 

So, here is what you do. 

  1. Open the view you want to change, or click "New Graphical View"
  2. Once the view has loaded open the development console for your browser (either press F12, or right-click -> 'Inspect') 
  3. Go to 'Sources' and right-click the folder with your tenant name and 'Search in all files':

    Then, search for the following: FUNCTION_NAME_TO_FUNCTION. This might take a minute or two. You'll get results that look something like this:

    In the file with the most hits, click any to open up the chunk. 

  4. In the open chunk, locate a function that contains "class a", and place a breakpoint AFTER the end of that function:
  5. Refresh the page, but do not close the debugger.
  6. When the debugger hits your breakpoint, find the console. 
  7. Locate your black trench-coat, 90s hacker glasses. Take a deep breath.
  8. Mandatory step. Say "I've hacked the mainframe" out loud. You may also whisper, "I'm in", if you are currently in an open office setup. 
  9. In the console, you can type a.ALL to see an array of all the functions:

    You can now see all the info required for a function to be included. 

  10. (Optional step) Open one of the functions and inspect the elements. Please take special note that the documentation in a RAW-string HTML document. 

    If you feel like it, you can either roll your eyes, do a facepalm or a similar display of "what the actual **bleep**". 

  11. From here, you can now push your functions to the class, and they will be legal in your graphical view. 
a.ALL.push({Function: 'ZCLEAN_AMOUNT', InTypes: 'decimal', Inputs: 1, Return: 'decimal', definition: 'amount Converter (UDF)', items: [{"key": ""}], prepared: true})​

Then, you also have to add the function to the FUNCTION_NAME_TO_FUNCTION dictionary:

a.FUNCTION_NAME_TO_FUNCTION['ZCLEAN_AMOUNT'] = {Function: 'ZCLEAN_AMOUNT', InTypes: 'decimal', Inputs: 1, Return: 'decimal', definition: 'amount converter (UDF)', items: [{"key": ""}], prepared: true}​

I'm not sure why, but I'm sure there are good, complex, technical reasons why (🙃).

  • Resume the script (F8) and let the view load finish:
  • You can now close the debugger. 
  • Your function is now legal, you can use it, deploy your view, and everything will compile and run without issue. 
    And why shouldn't it? SQL views and Graphical views both compile to the same HANA server. 
    The only reason graphical views behave differently is because SAP has actively restricted it. The only trick is to trick the graphical editor to not check. 

    Note: In all fairness, the Graphical view has to translate a graph to an SQL query, so there has to be some logic / special handling. Buuuuuut the list has remained static for over a year, so at this point it is just negligence.

So, that's it! 
And please note you can add any number of functions while the debugger is open. 
Both your own, or any function from the function reference that you need. 
Want to use JSON_QUERY functions for JSON extractions? No problem. Add it in, deploy, enjoy. 

Note: Be careful to add window functions, or aggregate functions, that are not supported. The view may compile and deploy, but it may behave oddly. 

Unfortunately, you'll have to repeat this process every time you open a graphical view.
Opening a view that contains a jail-break function, without adding it to the legal list, will result in an error, and prevent you from deploying. 

4: Conclusion

So, congratulations for making it all the way through that. I've tried my best to hit a balance between technical details, without it becoming overwhelming. 

If you've any questions or if anything was unclear, please leave a comment and I'll either reply or update the post, to improve clarity. 

So, by now you've learned :

  1. How to access the HANA Cloud tenant, and create custom SQL objects to implement reusable code. 
  2. Allow your spaces to consume said objects in your modelling
  3. Jail-break the graphical view editor, to include missing functions, without waiting 6 years for a feature request. 

Happy reading, happy coding and I hope you had a wonderful easter.

entry.title: Season 10 of Let's Talk Data Podcast Series: A focus on the data professionals that make it happen
entry.content:
Season 10 of SAP Let’s Talk Data is built for practitioners who make data work. We’re shifting from high-level storytelling to hard-earned, technical lessons from engineers, scientists, stewards, and architects who design, operate, and scale real-world data systems. Expect deep, operational conversations about the challenges, trade-offs, and best practices that turn data into repeatable, observable deliverables.

 What we’ll dig into

We will cover topics like your data platform architecture, data engineering at scale, (master) data quality and observability, data modelling from data practitioners and partners.

  • Governance and security: privacy-preserving analytics, metadata management, access policy enforcement, cataloging, encryption, and auditability in regulated environments.
  • Organizational and delivery practices: cross-functional workflows between platform, product, and analytics teams, prioritization under competing SLAs, and techniques for reducing friction between ops and research.

Here is a look at the first episodes

  • A Day in the Life of a Data Steward — Behind the Scenes of Trusted Data

What does a day in the life of a data steward really look like? This podcast pulls back the curtain on the people who keep enterprise data trustworthy, usable, and compliant. We will discuss how these data steward navigate through the rhythms of their day—triaging data incidents, curating metadata, negotiating business definitions, enforcing access controls, and coordinating fixes across engineering, analytics, and business teams. We explore both process realities and the best practices in place to ensure trust.

  • All in on AI: SAP BDC and SAP Business AI - coming soon

Leona Hasani is an SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) expert who specializes in all things data analysis and data management for BDC. In this podcast, she will dive deep into how BDC can help a data scientist leverage all the BDC features and technologies to maximize the value. If you are a data scientist, work with one, or in general, manage some form of the data environment in your team, then this podcast will help you understand all the tools available to you when using SAP Business Data Cloud.

Join us!

If you build data pipelines, ship analytics products, steward sensitive data, or operate ML systems, this season is engineered to deliver actionable guidance you can adapt to your stack and scale.

Subscribe to follow Season 10 (on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, YouTube, and more) and continue the conversation with the Data Professionals Community

entry.title: Welcome to Season 10 of SAP Let’s Talk Data!
entry.content:
Season 10 of SAP Let’s Talk Data is built for practitioners who make data work. We’re shifting from high-level storytelling to hard-earned, technical lessons from engineers, scientists, stewards, and architects who design, operate, and scale real-world data systems.

This season we’re putting the spotlight on the people who make data work: the data professionals shaping how businesses run. From data engineers and scientists to stewards, analysts, and architects, these roles don’t operate in isolation. Together they balance trust, governance, scale, and speed to turn data into real business impact.

Season 10 is about how ideas become impact. Expect candid conversations with practitioners who lead data operations, build analytics products, steward sensitive information, and translate insights into action. We’ll highlight real challenges—conflicting priorities, organizational friction, and technical trade-offs—and lessons learned from teams that turned those challenges into advantages. Whether you’re building data models, owning data quality, or shaping strategy, these episodes are designed to give you usable perspectives, actionable tips, and a clearer view of how data professionals connect to the broader business mission.

Because data professionals aren’t just supporting the business, they’re driving it forward.

Here is a look at the first episodes

  • A Day in the Life of a Data Steward — Behind the Scenes of Trusted Data

What does a day in the life of a data steward really look like? This podcast pulls back the curtain on the people who keep enterprise data trustworthy, usable, and compliant. We will discuss how these data steward navigate through the rhythms of their day—triaging data incidents, curating metadata, negotiating business definitions, enforcing access controls, and coordinating fixes across engineering, analytics, and business teams. We explore both process realities and the best practices in place to ensure trust.

  • All in on AI: SAP BDC and SAP Business AI - coming soon

Leona Hasani is an SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) expert who specializes in all things data analysis and data management for BDC. In this podcast, she will dive deep into how BDC can help a data scientist leverage all the BDC features and technologies to maximize the value. If you are a data scientist, work with one, or in general, manage some form of the data environment in your team, then this podcast will help you understand all the tools available to you when using SAP Business Data Cloud.

Join us!

Subscribe to follow the season and join the conversation as we lift the curtain on the people and practices powering data-driven organizations.

  • Spotify
  • Apple Podcasts
  • YouTube
  • And more…

What further connection for collaboration and growth?

Join the conversation with the Data Professionals Community!

The group welcomes perspectives across all data and AI topics. These types of projects deliver their greatest value when a team of colleagues work together towards the final solution. Thus, the spirit of this community.

entry.title: From Raw Data to Data Products: End-to-End Implementation using SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
This is my first blog and I will walks through a practical implementation of how data from SAP S/4HANA can be ingested into the Object Store, transformed into a business-ready Data Product within SAP Datasphere, and securely shared via the BDC Cockpit to Databricks using delta-enabled data sharing. Along the way, we will cover the prerequisites, step-by-step setup, and key considerations to successfully build and share a Delta Share Data Product in SAP Business Data Cloud.

Architecture illustrates how data flows from SAP S/4HANA into the Object Store, is transformed into a Data Product within SAP Datasphere, and is then shared via BDC Cockpit to Databricks using delta-enabled data sharing.

Pre-requisite:

  1. If your SAP Datasphere tenant is part of an SAP Business Data Cloud formation, then you can create data products for SAP Business Data Cloud.
  2. A Data Provider Profile should be created.
  3. An Object Store Space should be created and the required roles should be assigned.

Object Store Space

The Object Store space has been created and deployed in datasphere, and the required roles have been assigned to create replication flows.

Replication Flow

Create a replication flow for the I_PRODUCT table in the Object Store space, with the table load type set to Initial and Delta.

Delta Table

This is generated after the successful execution of the replication flow.

Data Provider Profile

To create a Delta Share data product, you must create a data provider profile with a dedicated visibility option called Formations.

  1. From the Data Sharing Cockpit, go to the My Data Provider Profile app, click Create Data Provider Profile to open the data provider page.
  2. Fill out your profile.
  3. Select the visibility option Formations.
  4. Enter a Namespace. A prefix based on your landscape's name is automatically generated to ensure consistency.
  5. Once you've created your Formations profile, you can create a Delta Share data product.

Creating a Delta Share Data Product

  1. In Data Sharing Cockpit > My Data Products, click Create Data Product.
  2. Enter the Business Name and the mandatory Technical Name of the data product.
  3. Fill out the Product Details.

As you're creating a data product as a Formations provider, the matching context is automatically assigned by default. 

Similarly, the Data Shipment is automatically set by default to Integrated Delivery, and the Delivery Mode to Delta Share. You cannot change these values.

  1. In the Pricing section, the Contract Type is also set by default to Free, and you cannot change it either.

Create Data Product
Select the Object Store Space.

 Context in new Data product will display the data provider name.

Shipment types: Integrated Delivery

The data is copied directly into the space that the consumers select.

Add Product Artifacts
Select the space where your local tables containing the data are included. The Artifact Space is automatically set to SAP HANA DATA LAKE storage type instead of regular HANA Cloud spaces.

Note: The SAP HANA DATA LAKE spaces don't contain any views so you can only add local tables and Only tables with supported column data types are displayed.

The lifecycle state of the new data product is set to Draft. To make it available for consumers, select the data product from the page My Data Products.

Click Switch Status to change the status to "Listed".

Status of the Data Product will be updated to Listed

Catalog users will be able to search for and evaluate these data products.

  • When the catalog is accessed from an SAP Datasphere system, users can install the data product to a space.
  • When the catalog is accessed from an SAP Business Data Cloud system, users can share the data product to a target system.

If at a later time you change the lifecycle status of a data product to Delisted, the data product will not be available in the catalog anymore. If the data product is installed to a space or shared to a target system, users won't be able to uninstall the data product or remove the shares until its status is changed back to Listed. 

Note: You can delete Delta Share data products once they are uninstalled from all SAP Datasphere spaces and are no longer shared with any tenants.

When you delete a Delta Share data product, the system also deletes the corresponding data share tables from SAP HANA DATA LAKE spaces.

Please note that once you've deleted a data product, you cannot create a new one with the same technical name.

SAP Business Data Cloud
Go to BDC Cockpit and search for data product.

Make sure you can share a data product by checking the following statuses:
The data product's release status is Active, its lifecycle status is Active, and its functional status is Current.

Share Data Product

Context:

Please remember that data products can only be shared to different systems within the same formation. You're not allowed to share them to the same system they were created from. If the target system you want to select is the same system that created the data product, use that system's native sharing capabilities instead. 

  1. In the side navigation area, choose (Catalog & Marketplace) > (Search).
  2. On the catalog search page, use the filters or the search to find the data product you want and choose it to open its details page.

In the dialog, choose Add Target to share the data product.

Choose the system you want to share to. If only one target is available, it's automatically selected. Here select Databricks system

Enter a share name
The name that you enter is the name that users will see in the SAP Databricks, and it cannot be changed after the data product is shared. Select the default workspace. You can select to share the data product with up to five workspaces, including the default workspace.

Choose Share
A message appears letting you know that the share process has started. As the share process runs, you'll receive a notification letting you know that the process started and a notification of when it finishes with the result. You'll also see that the Manage Access dialog continually refreshes until the share process finishes.

Databricks
Login to Databricks

Your data product is shared and its share status is updated and visible in the catalog search results. The name that you entered is the name that users will see in SAP Databricks, and it cannot be changed. If you need to change the name, you must delete the share setting for the data product and then share it to the target system again.

In conclusion, SAP Business Data Cloud provides a robust and scalable framework to transform raw data into business-ready insights. By leveraging Object Store for data ingestion, SAP Datasphere for modeling, and seamless data sharing to platforms like Databricks, organizations can unlock the full potential of their data. This approach not only ensures performance and governance but also enables cross-platform analytics in a consistent and efficient manner.

SAP Datasphere SAP Business Data Cloud SAP Databricks Object Store on SAP BTP

entry.title: Hybrid Data Modeling in SAP BDC: a simple Framework for product-oriented architecture
entry.content:
Intro

When facing a transformation project towards SAP BDC, the real question should be where each piece of logic belongs in a product-oriented architecture: close to the source as a primary data product, or in Datasphere as a derived one.

That boundary depends on semantics, ownership, and the BW starting point — especially whether you are on BW/4HANA PCE or BW 7.5 PCE. This is why the unit of exchange matters.

In BDC, the architectural shift is from objects to data products, and that change has direct consequences for how modeling decisions should be made.
BW PCE and Datasphere are not competing layers. They serve different purposes, and the strongest architectures place each logic block where it creates the most semantic value, the least technical debt, and the clearest ownership.
For those who prefer an infographic, here is a visual summary:

Where You Start Matters

Not all BW landscapes enter BDC from the same position, and the hybrid strategy changes depending on the BW release you run.
If you are on BW/4HANA PCE, you are in the cleanest architectural position. Your objects are already BW/4HANA-compliant — ADSOs, Composite Providers, InfoObjects — and the Data Product Generator can work with them directly. From there, the main decision is which BW PCE objects should become certified primary data products and which logic should be rebuilt natively in Datasphere. BW/4HANA PCE is supported at least until 2040, so there is no need to rush the architecture.
If you are on BW 7.5 PCE, the situation is different. BW 7.5 mainstream maintenance ends in 2027, with extended support available until 2030 at a premium. That matters, but the bigger issue is the legacy object gap. Many traditional BW 7.5 objects — classic Info-Cubes, Multi-Providers, older 3.x objects — cannot be used in BW/4HANA PCE and therefore will not be available in BDC after 2030. A lift-and-shift into PCE without addressing these objects simply delays the problem.

For BW 7.5, a credible hybrid strategy starts with an object-level assessment:

  • Which objects are BW/4HANA-compatible and can become data products now.
  • Which objects need conversion before they can be used in BDC.
  • Which logic should be rebuilt directly in Datasphere.

Standard SAP extractors such as 2LIS_02_ITM, 0CO_OM_CCA_9, and 2LIS_03_BF also require careful treatment when moving from BW 7.5 to BDC. They need a deliberate integration strategy, not an assumption of clean portability.

The Conceptual Shift: Stop Thinking in Objects, Start Thinking in Products

The key mindset shift in BDC is simple: the architectural unit is no longer the object, but the data product.
In traditional BW, consumers reach into queries, InfoProviders, and extractors. In BDC, the focus moves to data products that carry metadata, ownership, lineage, and a consumer contract. That is a deeper change than a terminology update.
This changes the modeling question. The relevant question is no longer whether something should stay in BW or move to Datasphere. The better question is whether that logic belongs in a primary data product or a derived one, and who should own it.
BDC uses three product types that matter in practice: 

  • SAP-managed data products are primary products delivered by SAP from source applications such as S/4HANA Public Cloud or RISE.
  • Customer-managed data products are primary products you create in Datasphere from BW-originated or other data, using your own domain logic.
  • Derived data products are secondary, consumer-oriented datasets that combine and enrich multiple primary sources.

The practical implication is straightforward: BW PCE is a strong producer of primary data products, while Datasphere is the place to curate, combine, and distribute derived ones. The boundary between the two is a product contract, not a migration line.

AI-Readiness and Planning do not shift the paradigm

What about AI and Planning? they would deserve their own article, but bottom line they confirm the principle.
AI does not introduce a new modeling principle; it raises the quality bar.

The same boundary rules that govern reporting also govern AI: preserve semantic correctness and lineage in the primary product layer, then publish governed derived products for consumption by analytics, SAP-native AI services, SAP Databricks, or other platforms accessible via BDC Connect and zero-copy sharing.

The same applies to Planning, your planning processes can succeed only if their baseline data layer is consistent, reliable, curated. This of course is not enough to have outstanding budgeting and forecasting processes, but it's mandatory.
Those reinforce that overall framework it's not only a matter of layers or platforms, it's a matter of having a governed ecosystem able to produce data products that can capture the whole complexity and completeness of business semantics.

What BW Does Best

BW PCE remains the right place for logic that depends on SAP semantics and needs deep built-in handling.
Currency translation is a good example. Multi-step translation paths, exchange rate types, fiscal-year-aware conversion, and runtime target currency selection are native BW strengths. Datasphere can reproduce this, but only by rebuilding logic that BW already handles well.
Delta extraction is another example. For many SAP source systems, especially in BW 7.5 landscapes, extraction logic is not a trivial technical layer. It often contains business rules that should remain stable and governed. In many cases, the right approach is to preserve that extraction logic, then expose the result as a governed data product for Datasphere consumption.
Non-cumulative key figures are also a BW strength. Stock quantities, headcount, and open items depend on exception aggregation behaviors such as MIN, MAX, and LAST. Those patterns don't have yet a similar coverage by the Datasphere Analytical Model.
InfoObject-based master data governance is another area where BW still has the edge. Time-dependent attributes, compounding, and SID-based joins provide referential integrity that Datasphere’s more flexible dimension model does not automatically replicate.

What Datasphere Does Best

Datasphere is not a replacement for BW. It is the right place for breadth, composition, and domain ownership.
Its strongest use case is the creation of derived data products. A Finance product from BW, an HR product from SuccessFactors, and an external benchmark source can be combined in Datasphere into a single analytical model such as cost per FTE by region. BW was not designed for this kind of cross-domain composition.
Datasphere is also the right place for domain-driven ownership. Its space model supports a data mesh operating model, where Finance, Supply Chain, and HR can each own and govern their own derived products independently. That gives teams clear accountability and reduces dependency on a central modeling team.
Consumption-layer modernization belongs here as well. New analytical models should be built in Datasphere, not as additional BW queries. The gap with BW query semantics is still real, but building new consumption artifacts in BW PCE only adds more logic to a layer that will eventually need to be unwound.

SAP-managed data products also need to be part of the decision. If SAP already provides a product that covers the domain adequately, that is often the right starting point. In that case, neither a custom BW model nor a custom Datasphere model should be the default answer.

A Simple Decision Framework

A good hybrid design becomes much easier if you ask three questions for each piece of logic.

1. Does this logic produce a primary or derived product?

If the logic extracts governed SAP data, applies BW-specific semantics, manages currency conversion, or handles delta logic, it is producing a primary product. It should stay as close to the authoritative source as possible.
If the logic combines multiple primary products or enriches SAP data with non-SAP sources, it is producing a derived product. That belongs in Datasphere.

2. How many consumers depend on it?

A small experimental dashboard can move quickly. A reconciled P&L report or a statutory close process cannot. The more consumers depend on the logic, the more important it is to validate equivalence before switching over.
That is where the data product contract helps. You can publish both versions, validate them in parallel, and move consumers gradually instead of forcing a hard cutover.

3. What does the legacy object inventory look like?

This is the critical BW 7.5 question. Classic InfoCubes, MultiProviders, and 3.x-era objects cannot be used in BW/4HANA PCE and therefore have no path into BDC after 2030.
Before you design a hybrid architecture on BW 7.5, you need an object compatibility assessment. That assessment will tell you whether BW PCE can still serve as a short-term primary product layer for a given domain, or whether that domain should move to Datasphere-native modeling sooner.

Where Projects usually break

Naive migration usually fails in the same places.
BW query semantics do not survive simple translation. Exception aggregation, non-cumulative key figures, and variable-driven dynamic filters do not map cleanly to Datasphere Analytical Models. The result is often subtle numeric differences that only appear under specific hierarchy settings or filter combinations.
Delta capture is defined at the source and is not reversible downstream. The source ADSO determines what can be loaded incrementally into Datasphere, and that cannot be fixed later. If delta handling is wrong at the beginning, the downstream design will inherit that mistake.
The legacy object gap remains a major risk in BW 7.5 landscapes. If you lift and shift without addressing object compatibility, the issue simply reappears later at higher cost.
SAP-managed data products create another common trap. They remain immutable, and custom Z-tables are not currently supported in them. Any extension pattern involving custom fields or tables should therefore be designed as a customer-managed derived product from the start.
Authorization is another area that requires redesign, not configuration. BW analysis authorizations do not have a direct one-to-one equivalent in Datasphere Data Access Control, so security needs to be treated as its own workstream.

Closing the Loop

The strongest BDC architectures treat the boundary between BW and Datasphere as a product contract, not a migration deadline.
Some logic belongs close to the source because it carries SAP semantics that are difficult to reproduce elsewhere. Some logic belongs in Datasphere because it combines sources, serves a domain, and creates new analytical value. SAP-managed products should be used whenever they already cover the need. The job of the architect is to place each piece of logic where it creates the best semantic result.
That is the real shift in BDC: not faster migration, but better placement. Not object thinking, but product thinking. And once that shift is made, the architecture becomes much clearer, much more defensible, and much easier to evolve.

entry.title: Configuring Single Sign-On (SSO) for SAP Datasphere using SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS)
entry.content:
In today’s enterprise landscape, managing secure and seamless access across multiple applications is not just a convenience - it’s a necessity. As organizations expand their SAP ecosystem, implementing a centralized authentication mechanism becomes critical for both user experience and security.

In this blog, I will walk through in detail on how Single Sign-On (SSO) can be effectively implemented for SAP Datasphere using SAP Cloud Identity Services, specifically leveraging the SAP Identity Authentication Service, integrated with OKTA. Microsoft Active Directory integration follows similar procedure.

What is Single Sign-On (SSO)?

Single Sign-On (SSO) is an authentication mechanism that allows users to log in once and gain access to multiple applications without being prompted to log in again.

Instead of managing multiple credentials, users authenticate through a trusted identity provider, which securely handles access across integrated systems.

Key Objective:

  • One Identity
  • One Login
  • Multiple Secure Access Points

What is SAP Datasphere?

SAP Datasphere is SAP’s next-generation cloud-based data warehousing solution that enables organizations to unify, analyze, and manage data across landscapes.

It provides:

  • Seamless data integration from SAP and non-SAP sources
  • Advanced analytics and data modeling capabilities
  • Business-ready semantic layers

Given its critical role in enterprise data strategy, secure and efficient user access becomes essential.

What are SAP Cloud Identity Services?

SAP Cloud Identity Services is SAP’s central identity management platform designed to handle authentication, authorization, and identity lifecycle management across SAP and non-SAP applications.

It consists of two main components:

  • Identity Authentication Service (IAS)
  • Identity Provisioning Service (IPS)

This blog focuses on IAS as the authentication engine for SSO.

What is SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS)?

SAP Identity Authentication Service is a cloud-based identity provider that manages user authentication and provides secure access to SAP applications.

It supports:

  • SAML 2.0, OAuth, OpenID Connect
  • Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
  • Conditional authentication policies

Architecture Overview: SSO with SAP IAS as Proxy

In this implementation:

  • IAS acts as a Proxy Identity Provider
  • Authentication requests are redirected to OKTA
  • IAS validates and passes authentication back to SAP Applications
  • Email address is used as the Unique User Identifier (Attribute)

Flow Simplified:

  1. User accesses SAP Datasphere
  2. Redirected to IAS (Proxy Identity Provider)
  3. IAS forwards authentication to OKTA
  4. User logs in via corporate credentials
  5. IAS validates and grants access
  6. User gains seamless access to SAP Datasphere

Why use Email as the User Attribute?

Using email as a unique identifier ensures:

  • Global uniqueness across systems
  • Alignment with corporate identity standards
  • Simplified user mapping across SAP and non-SAP applications

Benefits of Single Sign-On (SSO)

  1. Enhanced User Experience
  • Eliminates repeated logins
  • Faster access to applications
  • Reduced password fatigue

      2. Improved Security

  • Centralized authentication control
  • Enables Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
  • Reduces attack surface from password reuse
  1. Increased Productivity
  • Less time spent on login issues
  • Streamlined access to enterprise tools

End-to-End Configuration Steps

SAP IAS – OKTA Integration

Step 1: Extract the IAS Tenant Metadata: Login IAS as Administrator -> Applications & Resources -> Tenant Settings -> Single Sign-On -> SAML 2.0 Configuration -> Download Metadata File

Step 2: Configure IAS Tenant in OKTA: Login OKTA Admin Portal as Administrator -> Click on the 'Use single sign on' - 'Add App' option -> Create New App -> Platform = Web | Sign on method = SAML 2.0 -> Create

Provide IAS Tenant Description -> Maintain Single Sign-On Endpoint URL from IAS (ACS End Point) as Single Sign-On URL |  Recipient URL | Destination URL. Audience Restriction -> IAS Domain. Name ID Format -> E-mail Address. Digest Algorithm -> SHA256 -> Save.

Navigate to the 'Sign On' tab -> Click on 'Identity Provider metadata' hyperlink -> Export Configuration Metadata into an XML file

Step 3: Configure OKTA as Trusted Corporate Identity Provider : Login IAS Portal as Administrator -> Click on Identity Providers -> Corporate Identity Providers -> Create

Go to SAML 2.0 Configuration -> Browse and upload the OKTA Metadata XML file -> Save

Step 4: SAP Datasphere - IAS Configuration:

Only a System Owner can perform Single Sign-On Administration activity in SAP Datasphere.

Assign the System Owner Privilege with valid email address

Navigate to Information -> Administration

Step 4.1: Go to Security Tab -> Click on Change icon -> Authentication Method -> Choose SAML Single Sign-On (SSO)

Step 4.2: Under SAML Single Sign-On (SSO) Configuration -> Download Service Provider Metadata -> Extract SAP Datasphere Tenant’s Metadata (Here SAP Datasphere is the Service Provider)

We will complete parallel configuration in IAS for SAP Datasphere and resume further steps for seamless verification process

Step 4.2.1: Configure SAP Datasphere Application in IAS

Open new browser session -> Login IAS Tenant as Administrator -> Applications & Resources -> Applications -> Create -> Provide Display Name -> Choose Type as SAP Datasphere Solution -> Protocol Type – SAML2.0

Go to SAML 2.0 Configuration -> Upload SAP Datasphere Metadata (Service Provider) extracted from Step 4.2

Maintain Subject Name Identifier with Email as Primary Attribute. It is optional to configure fallback attribute.

Default Name ID Format -> Email

By default, Conditional Authentication will be set with Identity Authentication as Default Identity Provider

Resuming Configuration in SAP Datasphere -> Administrator -> Security

 Step 4.3: Under SAML Single Sign-On (SSO) Configuration -> Upload Identity Provider Metadata -> Upload IAS Tenant Metadata extracted from Step 1

Step 4.4: Under Confirm that the mapping is working, provide the System Owner E-mail Address -> Verify Account -> Follow instructions as per prompt

Note: System Owner ID must exist in IAS with correct e-mail address maintained

Step 4.5: Click Save icon to enable SAML Single Sign-On -> Convert

Authentication Verification with IAS: Try login SAP Datasphere Tenant in an Incognito / Private Browser session. Authentication to happen by IAS.

 Enabling IAS as Proxy:

Go to Conditional Authentication of SAP Datasphere Application and change the Default Identity Provider to the OKTA Corporate Identity Provider created in Step 3.

Authentication Verification with IAS as Proxy : Try login SAP Datasphere Tenant in an Incognito / Private Browser session. Authentication to happen by OKTA via IAS.

Conclusion

Implementing SSO for SAP Datasphere using SAP Cloud Identity Services - SAP Identity Authentication Service provides a robust, scalable, and secure authentication framework.

By configuring IAS as Proxy for Corporate Identity Provider, organizations can achieve a unified authentication strategy that enhances both user experience and security posture.

Author’s Note

This implementation reflects a real-world enterprise approach to consolidating authentication mechanisms across SAP landscapes using modern identity services.

If you're planning your Single Sign-On (SSO) journey or optimizing SAP Applications Architecture, this model serves as a strong foundation.

Referral Links

  • https://me.sap.com/notes/0002943651
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/connect-okta-to-identity-authentication/ba-p/13468665

#SAP #SingleSignOn #SSO #SAPDatasphere #SAPCloudIdentityServices #SAPIdentityAuthenticationService #SAPIAS #OKTA

entry.title: SAP BW Bridge or Data Product Generator (DPG): What would you choose
entry.content:
Ankit Sarkar 

  • Certified SAP Datasphere and Databricks Consultant
  • RG Technologies (2+ years of experience)

 

SAP BW Bridge and Data Product Generator (DPG)

Introduction

A common question arises - Should you expose your data for modern consumption, or should you migrate your BW system?

Organisations are moving from traditional on-premise BW systems to the cloud. So, the above question commonly arises.

The solution is BW Bridge and Data Product Generator (DPG). SAP offers two powerful options to migrate your BW system. You may see that they seem similar at first, but there are some huge differences between how they serve for your transformation journey.

SAP BW Bridge - Lift Your System to the Cloud: 

BW Bridge provides a way to migrate your existing BW system to the cloud with minimal disruption.

  • You can reuse the objects like DSOs, Info-Objects, and Transformations from the existing BW system.
  • You can entertain ABAP-based extraction logics.
  • You can keep on using classic extractors from SAP ECC and SAP S/4HANA.
  • Within SAP Datasphere, you can operate in a dedicated ABAP-enabled environment.

Use Cases : 

  • Companies use BW Bridge to move on-premise SAP/4HANA or SAP BW 7.x systems to SAP Datasphere without rebuilding data structures from scratch.
  • Organisations that excessively count on complex SAP S/API or ODP extractors, so with common methods, you can't connect directly to the cloud. Then you need the BW Bridge to connect with the cloud.
  • BW Bridge offers a staging area for complex transformations and cleaning before making the data available in Datasphere.

 SAP Data Product Generator (DPG) : 

You can use a different approach with DPG, instead of moving the whole system, DPG focuses on preparing your BW data usable in modern ecosystems like SAP Datasphere and Databricks.

  • You can replicate Info-Providers.
  • In tools like SAP Analytics Cloud or platforms like Databricks, you can do zero-copy consumption.
  • With the process chain, you can apply filters and schedule loads.
  • You can carry through full and delta data extraction.

Use Cases : 

  • When you need to migrate an on-premise BW system to a cloud system, you also need to keep the existing logics.
  • When you need to enable BW Info-providers in SAP Datasphere.
  • When you need to quickly bring out data models in Datasphere.
  • You need to perform AI/ML modelling with an external platform like Databricks, then you can share trusted, structured BW data with it.

 

Differences Between SAP BW Bridge and Data Product Generator (DPG) : 

AspectsSAP BW BridgeData Product Generator (DPG)
Core PurposeBW systems migrationReusable data products creation
Main FocusData Processing & Data WarehousingData Consumption & Data Sharing
Architecture RoleData Transformation and Data StorageData Reuse and Data Exposure
LayerModelling Layer or BackendBusiness Layer or Consumption
Data TransformationCan use data Transformation (ETL Logic)Cannot use Data Transformation
Data ModellingSupports full BW ModellingModelling is not used in DPG
Data Product CreationBW Bridge does not create any Data ProductData Product Creation is the core functionality of DPG
Data Storage It can store and manage dataIt uses the existing data
FlexibilityLess Flexible (BW Structure)More Flexible (Modern Approach)
ReusabilityLessHigh
Use CaseBW to Datasphere MigrationCurated Datasets sharing
Cloud Native To some extentCompletely 
Modern RelevanceLagacy MigrationFuture ready
Target UsersBW Developer or Data EngineersData Consumers or Business Users

Conclusion 

If you are shifting to SAP Datasphere, this is a common confusion about which service you need to use. 

In this Blog, I have explained some differences, use cases, and some practical examples or ways so you can decide which service you would use, whether you want to migrate your BW system or explore your data for a modern approach.

entry.title: Sizing the SAP Datasphere Object Store
entry.content:
What Do We Need To Size

The  SAP Datasphere Object Store consists of three features:

  1. The object store to hold data, that includes the data in the tables, metadata  logs, and back-up snapshots.
  2. The Apache Spark engine as the compute for all operations, like creating delta tables, transformations, and housekeeping.
  3. SAP HANA Native SQL on Files the SQL on files engine in SAP HANA, as an bridge between the object store and the relational world of SQL, that is used when reporting on data in the object store, when previewing it, when querying it in any fashion and more.

The SAP Datasphere Object Store meters three distinct, but dependent measures.

  • Object Store Storage - is the sum of all files and folders stored in the object store of an SAP Datasphere space.
  • Object Store Compute - is the total amount of 4 GB Memory hours (which combined CPU, Memory and temporary disk storage) used by all Spark jobs.
  • Object Store Requests - occur during all interactions with the object store, e.g. reading a file (get OPEN), writing a file (put CREATE), deleting a file (del DELETE) and many more (see documentation).

Data Volumes and Compression

We must be aware that the same records stored in a raw format, local tables (HANA) SAP HANA, or local tables (file) (stored as delta tables in SAP HANA Data Lake Files) use different amounts of storage. Compression rates can vary based on the actual data and its data types. One assumption that can be used is:

1 GB CSV (Raw) -> ~1/8 GB in HANA
1 GB HANA  -> 1.35 GB in delta tables

For the performance calculation, we will use the SAP HANA Data Lake Files (HDLF) data volume.

Object Store Storage

The total data footprint consists of the initially loaded data plus the incremental delta loads accumulated over time.

When data is loaded into a local table (file) using a Replication Flow, it is first written to an inbound buffer. Then a merge task has to be completed so that the data is moved into the underlying delta table.

Data files in delta tables are immutable. Any update, delete, or merge operation generates new files and record ‘remove file’ actions for the old ones. As a result, overwriting data increases storage usage because previous file versions must be retained. These older files are needed for delta extraction and must remain available until all delta subscribers have consumed their data. They must be removed later by a vacuum job. The amount of storages used by these old versions depends on whether the source provides change data (updates/deletes) or only new inserts

The object store storage can be calculated by combining the initially loaded data and adding the periodic load volume that is added per load, times the frequency of the periodic load and the version retention period. In the worst-case scenario, all files receive an update during a delta load, which would cause the table to be re-written with a slightly larger size. 

Here, SAP is working on multiple improvements to help minimize this growth, see the Roadmap Item 

The best-case scenario is when only inserts are delivered and files are never changed, in which case the table storage only grows by its added volume of data during a merge.

The object store keeps a backup of all deleted files for disaster recovery over a period of 14 days.

The versions and backup should level out eventually, when the retention periods are reached and a new version comes in, as an old version leaves the system.

Storage is metered hourly. That results in the fact that the monthly CU charged is not the size at the end of the month time hours in the month, it is rather the integral over time. For a linear graph, it’s the the average data size multiplied by the number of hours in the month (for estimations we assume 730h per month). Or in other words we need to estimate the average active data size in our system.

Object Store Compute

Object Store Compute is measured in blocks, based on 4 GB/h. This is because all Apache Spark configurations currently have a 1 CPU to 4 GB memory ratio.

Therefore, we need to estimate how many hours Apache Spark will run. This is the sum of hours spent on:

  • Task runs in the object store (spaces with storage type "SAP HANA Data Lake Files")
  • Deployments in the object store (spaces with storage type "SAP HANA Data Lake Files")
  • System Tasks
    • Statistic Updates 
    • Housekeeping jobs (like cleanup of logs)

For merge and transformation flow tasks, the required object store compute depends on many factors:

Data Characteristics

  • Volume - total data size drives memory and parallelism needs
  • Cardinality - high-cardinality columns (e.g. user IDs) affect shuffle size in groupBy/join
  • Skew - uneven key distribution causes some tasks to process far more data than others
  • Schema width - wide rows (many columns) increase serialization overhead
  • Write pattern - insert-only workloads produce fewer redundant files than upsert/delete-heavy ones, so Optimize is cheaper after them.

Transformation Type

  • Narrow vs. wide transformations - filter, map, select stay within a partition; groupBy, join, distinct, repartition cause shuffles (expensive)
  • Shuffle operations - triggered by wide transformations; move data across the network and write intermediate results to disk
  • User defined functions (UDF) - python UDFs especially are slow (JVM ↔ Python serialization); prefer built-in Spark SQL functions

Workload Configuration

  • Executor memory/cores - determine parallelism and whether spill to disk occurs
  • Dynamic allocation - lets Spark scale executors based on workload

For optimize, the required object store compute depends on:

  • Number of small files - the main cost driver. Each write operation (merge, load, upsert) appends new files and creates new versions. The more fragmented the table, the more spark workload optimize must use to compact them. This makes optimize costs grow with write frequency, not just data size.
  • Table size (total data volume) - larger tables take longer to compact even at the same file count.
  • Z-ORDER BY - if column co-location (Z-ordering) is applied, Optimize must physically reshuffle data across files to group related rows. This is significantly more compute-intensive than simple compaction.

For an overview on best practices on optimizing consumption of the object store see the SAP Note 3722983

Object Store Requests

Object store requests are created during any read and write interaction with the object store. Besides the Spark-based tasks we need to add the object store requests from the Replication Flow writing into the inbound buffer table and we need to consider data access by SQL on File (SoF) calls.

Object store requests will be the combination of:

  • Task runs in the object store (spaces with storage type "SAP HANA Data Lake Files")
  • Replication Flow writes into Inbound Buffer
  • Deployments in the object store (spaces with storage type "SAP HANA Data Lake Files")
  • SQL on Files (e.g. cross-space sharing to HANA spaces or Data Preview)

Factors that impact object store requests:

  • Access method to local tables (file) (delta/active) - delta access requires additional access to logs and versions, while the volume in delta is typically smaller

Merge

  • Data volume in Inbound Buffer - determines how many target files are candidates for rewrite
  • Insert-only vs update/delete - insert-only merge can append new files; updates/deletes require rewriting matched files. When only inserts occur, it skips expensive target file rewrite

SQL on File reads into HANA

  • Total number of files in local table (file) -  fewer large files vs. many small files (influenced by frequency of merge or optimize runs)
  • Partition pruning - filters on partition columns eliminate entire directories → fewer calls
  • Column pruning - only requested columns are fetched (fewer bytes, same file count)

HANA can be used to cache data for reads, especially for SAP Analytics Cloud. Related best practice is to create data marts in spaces with SAP HANA Cloud storage (local tables (HANA) or persisted views) for reporting scenarios with frequent use and SLAs requiring high-performance.  There are ways to mitigate the full reads in SAP Datasphere persisted views . You can partition views and lock partitions that don’t change, preventing unnecessary data read. To enable this, ensure that the partitions defined in the view are aligned with the partitions defined in the Local Table (File),  so that partition locking effectively avoids reading the underlying files.

Another alternative is to use local tables (HANA) instead of view persistency.  This allows the system to load only the deltas from the Local Tables (File), so it reads only the changes. As a result, the number of object store requests is significantly reduced.

SQL on Files object store requests run by queries (such as MDS queries, previews, etc.) are even more difficult to estimate, as there is typically no available data to base these calculations on. 

For an overview on best practices on optimizing consumption of the object store see the SAP Note 3722983

Input for sizing

  • Find your average active data size
  • Estimate how many small (<5GB), medium (5GB-50GB) and large (> 50GB) tables you have and what percentage of tables have changes, and which are insert-only
  • Define data availability and update frequency for tables based on end user requirements and object store consumption. More frequent merge runs increase object store consumption.
  • Determine the number of potential re-initializations per year, for example required for incompatible schema changes in connected sources
  • Make an assumption about data retention (housekeeping on data performed with delete and subsequent vacuum processes).

If you need support to come up with an estimate, reach out to your SAP contact (Sales, Pre-Sales, COE)

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) Onboarding
entry.content:
In this blog we will give a brief outline as to the onboarding process for SAP BDC, point to the many online resources and provide details on how you can get 1:1 assistance from an SAP onboarding specialist. 

Target audience

  • You may be a direct employee of the SAP customer who is tasked with provisioning the system.
  • You may be a third party consultancy, engaged by the SAP customer, who is tasked with provisioning the system.

Prerequisites

  1. The BDC license is purchased, and you have received the welcome email. After your organization has purchased SAP Business Data Cloud, the user designated as the contact person receives an invitation email with a link to SAP for Me. If you’re unsure about your registration as a contact person for SAP Business Data Cloud, contact your Sales Account Executive or the Customer Interaction Center (CIC).

    If you are from a third party of the SAP customer, then reach out to your contact on the SAP customer side. They must provide you with this email. Note: Do not open a support Case with SAP Support as they cannot resend or provide you with this email.

  2. You will also require an S-user with Super Admin or Cloud Admin access in SAP for Me. Refer to KBA 2492132 What is the difference between Super, Cloud, and User Administrator S-user IDs? - SAP for Me and more information available here Understanding S-Users and SAP for Me

Outline Steps

  1. Provisioning the BDC Cockpit is the first step and you cannot proceed to provision anything else until this step is completed. We provide step by step instructions in the Community Blog Part 1: Provisioning of Business Data Cloud through SAP4Me
  2. Then provision the BDC Core components of Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), Databricks as per your business requirements. Refer to steps in Provisioning SAP Business Data Cloud Applications

Resources

Before embarking on the provisioning process, it is important to educate yourself on the entire process.

  1. The Business Data Cloud Resource Center is your starting point for this journey



    There are many resources available here including: 
    1. the SAP BDC Onboarding Webcast which should be mandatory viewing for anyone who is about to provision SAP BDC.
    2. full video of the provisioning process here: Provisioning of SAP Business Data Cloud and its core components
    3. SAP BDC Onboarding – this is the official product documentation which explains in detail the entire provisioning process.
    4. SAP BDC Community Blog series here Introductory Guide to Provisioning of SAP Business Data Cloud – this has a series of blogs with screenshots showing the step by step process.

  2. If you still have questions or if you would like to have an SAP onboarding expert walk you through the provisioning process, you can book a 1:1 onboarding session with SAP via SAP Business Data Cloud -> “If you still have questions, request a 1:1 onboarding session here”.

  3. If you encounter technical issues during the actual provisioning process, then you may open a Support Case via the SAP for Me portal.
  4. If you have questions outside of SAP Technical Support area of responsibility or if you would like to leverage the knowledge and experience of other SAP BDC Administrators and users, post your questions to the SAP Business Data Cloud - SAP Community Q&A space.

Additional Resources

  • Official Product documentation here: SAP Business Data Cloud
  • SAP Community Blog SAP Business Data Cloud - From Provisioning to Intelligent Applications
  • SAP Community Blog SAP Business Data Cloud - FAQs
  • SAP Community Blog Rewiring of SAP Datasphere to SAP Business Data Cloud
  • KBA 3733691 SAP BDC Onboarding FAQ
  • KBA 2626344 Who is the SAP Account Manager/Executive for my company?
  • KBA 3705747 Provisioning failure due to duplicate host name during SAP Business Data Cloud Cockpit

entry.title: Building a Real AI Agent: Natural Language Modeling for SAP Datasphere with Vibe Coding
entry.content:
In my previous article, Using Claude Code and Natural Language to Create SAP Datasphere Artifacts, I used Claude Code's natural language capabilities to automatically generate SAP Datasphere models — in that scenario, Claude Code itself was the Agent, understanding my intent, calling CLI scripts, and completing the modeling. But afterward, I kept thinking: what if I wanted an Agent of my own? One with a web interface that teammates could use, with freely extensible tools? So I decided: use Vibe Coding to build my own Agent.

1. From "Using an Agent" to "Building an Agent"

Let me clarify the difference between these two things.

In the previous article, the workflow was: I told Claude Code in the terminal "create a sales data table," and Claude Code understood my intent, assembled the parameters, called JS scripts, and built the table. The Agent here was Claude Code — its Agent Loop, tool calling, and context management were all built by Anthropic, ready to use out of the box.

But this had several limitations: only I could use it in the terminal, with no way to share it with colleagues unfamiliar with the command line; the tool extension and orchestration logic was locked inside Claude Code, beyond my customization; and if I wanted to switch LLM backends or add RAG retrieval capabilities, there was no way in.

So this time, my goal was to implement every component of the Agent myself — LLM calls, tool registration, Agent Loop, streaming communication, Web UI — and plug in the previously written JS skill scripts as tools. The development approach? Still Vibe Coding, still conversing with Claude Code, except this time its role shifted from "Agent" to "development partner helping me build an Agent."

2. What Is an AI Agent? Let's Talk Design Patterns

Before getting hands-on, it's worth understanding the major design patterns for AI Agents. These aren't academic concepts — they're choices you must make when building an Agent, and they determine how your Agent reasons and orchestrates tool calls.

2.1 ReAct: Reason-Act-Observe

ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) is currently the most widely used Agent pattern. Its workflow is intuitive:

Reason (Which tool should I call?) → Act (Call the tool) → Observe (See the result) → Reason again (Is that enough? What's next?)

This loops until the task is complete. This is exactly the core pattern my Agent uses. The LLM doesn't give a one-shot answer — it continuously reasons, executes, and reasons again in a loop. For example, when a user says "create a sales data table with ID, name, and amount," the Agent's inner monologue might be:

  • Reason: The user wants a table, I should call the create_local_table tool
  • Act: Assemble parameters, call the tool
  • Observe: Success returned, table created
  • Reply to user: Table created with ID, NAME, and AMOUNT columns

2.2 Function Calling / Tool Use: Giving LLMs Hands and Feet

LLMs can only generate text on their own. But through the Tool Use mechanism, you can provide the LLM with descriptions of available tools (name, parameters, purpose), and the LLM can output not just text but structured tool-calling instructions — "I want to call create_local_table with parameters {name: 'SALES', columns: '...'}." An external program executes the call and feeds the result back to the LLM.

This is OpenAI's function calling protocol and the foundational capability of most Agent frameworks. My Agent is built on this mechanism: existing scripts are registered as tools, and the LLM decides when to call which one.

2.3 Reflection: Self-Review and Error Correction

The Reflection pattern lets an Agent look back at its own output to judge "was that right?" and correct course if not. It typically doesn't require extra code logic — it can be implemented through System Prompt instructions. For example, you can tell the Agent "if the model name already exists, automatically append a suffix and retry," and the Agent will autonomously adjust its strategy upon observing a 409 conflict, rather than throwing the error directly to the user.

2.4 Other Patterns: Planning and Multi-Agent

Planning (plan-then-execute) has the Agent formulate a multi-step plan before executing step by step. Suitable for complex tasks like "build me a data warehouse with three related tables and an analytic model."

Multi-Agent has multiple Agents collaborate, each with its own role. For example, one understands requirements, one writes SQL, one reviews quality. Suitable for complex workflows requiring specialization.

For this project, I chose the ReAct + Tool Use + Reflection combination — sufficient for current needs, simple enough architecturally, and evolvable toward Planning or Multi-Agent later.

3. From Requirements to Plan: Claude Code's Plan Mode

With design patterns decided, it was time to execute. I opened Claude Code and described the requirements in natural language: six existing JS CLI scripts need to be upgraded into an AI Agent framework with a Web UI, using FastAPI for the backend, Next.js for the frontend, calling LLMs through an OpenAI-compatible interface, and keeping existing scripts unchanged by calling them via Python subprocess.

Claude Code didn't start writing code immediately. It first entered Plan Mode — generating a complete architecture plan for my review and approval. This process is important: it ensures the AI and I are aligned on "what to build," rather than going off in different directions.

Here are the key parts of the plan Claude Code generated:

Tech Stack

  • Frontend: Next.js + Tailwind CSS + TypeScript
  • Backend: FastAPI + Python
  • LLM: HyperSpace LLM Proxy (OpenAI-compatible API)
  • JS Skills: Keep existing code, call via subprocess

Project Structure

dsp-agent/
├── frontend/          # Next.js frontend
│   └── src/
│       ├── app/       # Pages
│       ├── components/# Chat components
│       ├── lib/       # API client, SSE stream parser
│       └── types/     # TypeScript types
├── backend/           # FastAPI backend
│   └── app/
│       ├── agent/     # Agent Loop, LLM client, System Prompt
│       ├── api/       # REST routes (chat, tools, health)
│       ├── tools/     # Tool base class, registry, Datasphere tools
│       └── models/    # Data models
└── skills/            # Existing JS Skills (untouched)

Phased Implementation

  • Phase 1: Backend Agent core (LLM client, Tool system, Agent Loop, API routes)
  • Phase 2: Frontend chat interface (SSE streaming, message rendering, tool call visualization)
  • Phase 3: Enhancements (session management, plugin system, vector DB interface reserved)

I reviewed the plan, adjusted a few details, and only after confirmation did it begin implementing module by module.

4. Vibe Coding in Practice: An Afternoon of Conversation

With the architecture blueprint in hand, it was time to implement. The entire process was done through Vibe Coding — describing requirements in natural language in the Claude Code CLI, advancing module by module.

4.1 Solving a UX Problem First: SSE Streaming

Before building specific modules, I thought through a UX problem: a user sends one message, but the Agent might need several rounds of tool calls, each taking tens of seconds. If we wait for everything to finish before returning one big result, users stare at a blank page — terrible experience.

The solution was SSE (Server-Sent Events). Simply put, the backend doesn't batch up a big result to return — it pushes an event stream in real time, like a live broadcast. I defined five event types: AI starts outputting text (text_delta), starts calling a tool (tool_call_start), tool returns results (tool_call_result), error (error), and done (done).
